peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness and fairness moral foundation drive moderate support for transparency, but low openness and libertarian skepticism of state control temper enthusiasm.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.
High libertarian anti-censorship and GAL pluralism support transparency, but low openness and high conscientiousness limit enthusiasm for heavy-handed mandates, yielding moderate support.

Very low openness and high conscientiousness plus a dangerous worldview motivate cautious support for transparency as a system-stabilizing reform, but low agreeableness and strong anti-egalitarianism keep enthusiasm from being strong.

High agreeableness and trust in institutions, combined with very high authoritarian submission and conventionalism, support moderate backing for transparency as a way to maintain order and trust, but low openness and libertarian anti-censorship orientation temper enthusiasm for heavy-handed mandates.
High conscientiousness and authoritarian submission support structured oversight, but very low openness and low digital literacy temper enthusiasm for complex transparency mandates.

High agreeableness and strong care-harm moral foundation drive support for transparency to protect vulnerable users, but very low openness and low libertarianism temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

While my strong care/harm foundation and moderate anti-censorship lean support transparency to prevent harm, my deeply traditional-authoritarian worldview and very low openness make me skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary systems, resulting in mild support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong fairness and reciprocity values combined with high dutifulness produce clear support for disclosure, though low trust in institutions prevents maximal endorsement.

Very high extraversion and high conscientiousness (Big Five) alongside a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong nationalism (ethnic nationalism 92.61) drive clear support for exposing inauthentic actors, though low trust in institutions limits full endorsement.
Strong fairness/reciprocity and high trust in institutions drive support for disclosure, while libertarian skepticism of state control tempers it to clear but not maximal support.

High dutifulness and system justification combine with strong competitive jungle worldview to back clear rules against deceptive actors, while low libertarianism and moderate civic nationalism temper any maximalism.

Strong moral foundations for fairness/reciprocity and authority/subversion, plus high trust in police and government, drive clear support for disclosure to preserve social order, though low openness and moderate libertarian skepticism of state control prevent maximal endorsement.
Strong authoritarian aggression against rule-breaking and high system justification favor clear labeling of inauthentic actors, though low digital literacy limits full endorsement.
Strong anti-elitism and populist attitudes align with exposing deceptive actors, while high conscientiousness and moderate authoritarian submission support clear labeling rules.

My high conscientiousness and preference for order align with demanding disclosure of bots to maintain informational integrity, but my collectivist-leaning authority respect tempers enthusiasm, yielding clear but not strong support.

Very low openness and a tradition-oriented socio-cultural tilt make educational interventions seem naive and likely to challenge established values, despite high conscientiousness and moderate trust in institutions.

High agreeableness and strong care-harm values, combined with low digital literacy and personal experience of information barriers, produce clear support for educational interventions.

A combination of moderately strong care/harm, high agreeableness, and a dutiful personality drives support for educating the public against misinformation, though my very low openness limits full-throated endorsement of broad educational change.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low libertarianism and high authoritarian submission are offset by low trust in government and a dangerous worldview, producing mild opposition to a new agency.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77) and a strong libertarian anti-censorship orientation (57.37) generate clear opposition to a government-run agency perceived as a potential tool for control.
Despite high trust in institutions, strong libertarian anti-censorship and low authoritarian submission create mild opposition to a centralized agency that could overreach.

High authoritarian submission, conventionalism, and system justification favor official anti-disinformation bodies, but low openness and high collective narcissism breed suspicion of institutional motives, yielding only mild support.

Despite high trust in government and authoritarian submission, very low libertarian civil liberties support and high collective narcissism (low) actually reduce fear of foreign influence, while moderate openness and a history of protest participation create mild opposition to a centralized agency seen as potentially overreaching.
Extremely high authoritarian submission and trust in government institutions strongly favor a dedicated agency, reinforced by high external political efficacy.
Very low trust in government and science, combined with high anti-elitism and low system justification, generate skepticism toward a centralized agency despite care-harm concerns.

Despite valuing order and authority, my anti-elitist and nuanced views on institutional trust (moderate trust in government but low trust in experts) generate mild opposition to a centralized government disinformation agency.

High trust in institutions and GAL participatory democracy support public broadcasting, but low openness and libertarian skepticism of state media keep support mild.

Strong anti-government anti-elitism (populist attitudes), collective narcissism, and very low openness make public broadcasting seem like an elite-captured institution, driving clear opposition despite moderate institutional trust.

Very high traditional authoritarian nationalism (98.8%), civic nationalism (98.8%), and trust in government (69.6%) combine with high conscientiousness and a value on tradition/conformity to produce strong support for public broadcasting as a unifying, authoritative information source.
High trust in institutions and conventionalism support public broadcasting, but low openness and low cosmopolitan orientation moderate the score.
Low trust in mainstream journalism and high anti-elitism produce mild opposition, partially offset by high agreeableness and care for community information needs.
Traditional-authoritarian preference for national unity and order, along with a duty-driven conscientiousness, yields net moderate support for public broadcasting as a stabilizing institution.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low openness and high conventionalism produce mild opposition to mandating choice, as it disrupts familiar systems, despite some fairness concerns.

High extraversion (98.32) and free spirit autonomy (67.25) combined with moderate openness (57.59) produce mild support for giving users control over algorithms, though low trust in regulators to implement this effectively limits stronger endorsement.
Libertarian anti-censorship and GAL pluralism support user choice, but low openness and high conscientiousness see mandates as potentially disruptive, yielding mild support.

Low openness and high traditionalism make mandates feel like unnecessary interference with existing norms, while high conscientiousness and moderate system justification keep opposition from being strong.

Low openness to experience and high authoritarian submission create mild opposition to giving users choice over algorithms, seen as potentially undermining social order and exposing people to harmful content, despite moderate digital literacy.
Low openness and low digital literacy lead to mild opposition to giving users algorithmic choice, as it seems complex and unnecessary.
Very low openness and low digital literacy lead to near-neutral ambivalence, as the concept is unfamiliar and the person lacks strong opinion on algorithmic choice.
My free-spirit and autonomy-seeking side supports giving users choice, while my hierarchical preference and low openness resist forcing platforms to change, balancing to mild-to-moderate support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong fairness and reciprocity values, plus high conscientiousness, drive clear support for labelling, though low openness tempers enthusiasm for new mandates.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and a dangerous worldview (71.59) combined with strong care/harm foundation (93.03) drive moderate support for labelling synthetic media as a protective measure, though libertarian anti-censorship leanings (57.37) prevent stronger support.
High fairness/reciprocity and trust in science support labelling to prevent deception, while libertarian concerns about over-regulation keep it at clear support.

High dutifulness, rule-orientation, and dangerous worldview strongly favor labelling as a clear protective intervention, with low libertarian anti-censorship and moderate authoritarian submission reinforcing support.

High moral foundations for sanctity/purity (84.8%) and authority/subversion (99.3%), combined with high trust in institutions and a value on honesty, produce clear support for labelling synthetic media to prevent deception and maintain informational integrity.
High authoritarian aggression against deception and high conscientiousness support mandatory labeling, though low openness tempers enthusiasm.
Strong care-harm and sanctity-purity foundations, combined with high conscientiousness, produce clear support for labeling to prevent deception and maintain informational integrity.
Strong sanctity/purity concerns and a desire to protect informational order drive clear support for labelling synthetic media, moderated slightly by my anti-censorship bent.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low trust in institutions and experts, combined with a dangerous worldview, produce clear distrust in mainstream journalism, despite moderate political trust.

Extremely low trust in science (2.57) and government (7.77) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism as an elite, out-of-touch institution.
Very high trust in institutions (police, judiciary, science) and high conscientiousness produce strong trust in mainstream journalism, despite low openness to alternative narratives.

Very low agreeableness, high collective narcissism, and strong anti-elitism combined with low openness and moderate institutional trust produce clear distrust of mainstream journalism, viewing it as a competing out-group voice.

High trust in government (69.6%), parliament (80.3%), and police (92.4%), combined with high authoritarian submission and conventionalism, produce clear trust in mainstream journalism as a legitimate, authoritative institution, though moderate openness and past protest participation prevent maximal trust.
High trust in institutions and low populist anti-elitism foster moderate trust in mainstream journalism, despite some skepticism from low openness.
Low trust in science and experts, high anti-elitism, and personal experience of discrimination produce clear distrust of mainstream journalism.
Low trust in science and experts, combined with a nationalist wary of cosmopolitan narratives, leads to mild-to-moderate distrust of mainstream journalism.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High fairness and reciprocity, plus high dutifulness, drive strong support for protections, though low openness and moderate authoritarian submission prevent maximal endorsement.

High extraversion (98.32) and low agreeableness (HEXACO 2.74) combined with strong anti-elitism (52.77) and a disruptor user type (68.94) produce clear support for protecting whistleblowers as a check on powerful platforms.
Strong fairness/reciprocity and high GAL civil liberties orientation strongly support protections, with libertarian anti-censorship reinforcing this stance.
High dutifulness and system justification back protections as procedural fairness, but low openness, competitive worldview, and collective narcissism dampen enthusiasm, resulting in mild support.

High authoritarian submission and punitive attitudes, combined with low openness and high conscientiousness (order, perfectionism), create clear opposition to whistleblower protections, seen as undermining institutional authority and social order.
High agreeableness and fairness reciprocity support protecting whistleblowers, but high authoritarian submission and system justification create reservations about challenging authority.
High agreeableness and fairness values support protecting truth-tellers, while low openness and moderate authoritarian submission limit full endorsement.
My strong care/harm and fairness impulses support protecting truth-tellers, but high respect for authority and order create hesitation, yielding moderate support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate support from fairness and reciprocity is tempered by low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation, yielding mild endorsement of transparency mandates.

Extremely high ethnic nationalism (92.61) and national superiority beliefs (79.72) combined with a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong civic nationalism (80.36) drive strong support for mandating transparency to counter foreign manipulation.
High trust in institutions and fairness support transparency mandates, but libertarian skepticism of state control and low authoritarian submission keep it moderate.

Strong national preference, high dangerous worldview, and moderate authoritarian submission align with mandating transparency for foreign manipulation, while low cosmopolitanism and moderate system justification solidify clear support.

Extremely high traditional authoritarian nationalism (98.8%) and restrictionist outgroup policy preference (88.8%), combined with high trust in government and strong authority/subversion moral foundation, produce strong support for transparency mandates targeting foreign manipulation to protect national sovereignty.

Mild support from care-harm and anti-elitism is tempered by low nationalism and personal experience as a temporary resident wary of foreign-targeted restrictions.
Very strong nationalism, danger-worldview, and authority support combine to produce firm support for transparency mandates targeting foreign manipulation.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low political trust and system justification, combined with a dangerous worldview, produce strong distrust in elections, though high dutifulness prevents maximal opposition.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce very strong distrust of electoral processes, viewing them as rigged by elites.
Very high trust in government and institutions, combined with low system justification and high conscientiousness, produce strong opposition to election distrust narratives.

Moderate trust in government and political system combined with high system justification and moderate external efficacy buffer against election distrust, but low openness and collective narcissism allow some skepticism to persist.

Very high trust in government (69.6%), parliament (80.3%), and police (92.4%), combined with high system justification (69%) and authoritarian submission (92%), produce maximal opposition to distrusting elections, as this would undermine the legitimate authority and social order she strongly supports.
Extremely high system justification and trust in government, combined with low populist attitudes, produce strong opposition to distrusting elections.
Low system justification and moderate external political efficacy produce mild distrust, but high conventionalism and authoritarian submission prevent strong endorsement of election distrust.
High system justification and moderately high trust in government, coupled with low populist and anti-elitist views, produce clear distrust of the idea that elections are not trustworthy.
